title: The West can learn from Ukraine’s AI future vision beyond the battlefield
gist: Andrew D'Anieri argues Ukraine's nimble government, AI deployment expertise, and acute human-capital gap make it a live testing ground for an 'agentic state'—exemplified by Diia.AI, the first national-government AI agent (built on Google Gemini Flash 2.0)—offering Western democracies a fast-iterating model for AI-driven public services and AI-optimized defense procurement.

insights.claim: Ukraine launched Diia.AI in fall 2025, the first AI agent for a national government to provide bureaucratic services, built by Ukrainian firm Kitsoft on Google's Gemini Flash 2.0 model.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: First-mover national gov AI agent on a commercial LLM is a reference deployment and a demand signal for Google's models in govtech.
insights.quote: Diia.AI is the first AI agent for a national government to provide simple bureaucratic services to citizens
insights.locator: In fall 2025, the ministry launched Diia.AI

insights.claim: Acting Digital Transformation Minister Oleksandr Bornyakov is building an 'agentic state'—an ecosystem of AI agents performing bureaucratic functions.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: A whole-of-government agentic strategy points to a multi-year procurement pipeline for AI agents in public administration.
insights.quote: an ecosystem of AI agents to carry out a range of bureaucratic functions

insights.claim: Ukraine adopts AI for government services for the same reason it leads in AI weaponry: an acute human-capital gap that forces automation to manage complexity at scale.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: Labor scarcity, not ideology, is the deployment driver—suggesting aging/labor-short economies are the natural next adopters.
insights.quote: Agentic systems expand the ability to manage complexity and decision-making at a scale no workforce can match.
insights.locator: Ukraine is leaning into AI for government services

insights.claim: Ukraine's Brave1 defense accelerator gives startups grants, data, and software to scale defense products at speed, drawing European and American partners.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: Brave1 is a concrete partnership and dealflow channel into Ukrainian defense-tech for Western industry.
insights.quote: the country's Brave1 defense accelerator provides fledgling defense startups with grants, data, and software
insights.locator: Meanwhile, the country's Brave1 defense accelerator

insights.claim: The Ministry of Defense will use AI to process battlefield data and decide which drones to procure, aiming to maximize effectiveness and minimize corruption risk in procurement.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: AI-driven procurement is a novel, anti-corruption use case worth watching as a model for defense acquisition.
insights.quote: the Ministry of Defense will now rely on AI to process battlefield data and determine which drones it should procure

insights.claim: Ukraine is pursuing partially and fully autonomous frontline defenses to offset a manpower disadvantage, whereas US debates over autonomous weapons have caused major fissures between government and AI companies.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: Ukraine's faster autonomy adoption highlights a Western regulatory/ethical bottleneck that shapes the autonomous-weapons market.
insights.quote: debates about autonomous weapons systems have led to major fissures between the US government and AI companies
insights.locator: In the United States, debates about autonomous weapons

themes.theme: Lessons and risks for the West

insights.claim: Western governments could replicate Diia.AI by training a high-quality model on existing government data for a single use case (e.g., tax filing) with security guardrails, piloting narrowly to minimize risk and iterate, much like buying micro-quantities of drones for battlefield testing.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: A concrete, low-risk adoption playbook for govtech AI; narrow-use piloting is the actionable template.
insights.quote: Piloting an agent for one particular use minimizes risk and makes it easier to iterate and fine tune
insights.locator: It might be possible for Western governments

insights.claim: Rapid consumer-facing AI rollout during Ukraine's recovery will raise data-privacy and platform-security concerns among the public and experts, intensifying the innovation-versus-security debate.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: Privacy/security backlash is the key risk to the agentic-state model and to vendors building on government data.
insights.quote: concerns about data privacy and platform security will also surely crop up

insights.claim: Governments that fail to integrate AI-enabled services risk growing friction with increasingly AI-native populations and missing opportunities to shape the technology's development.
insights.speaker: Andrew D'Anieri
insights.so_what: Frames AI adoption as a competitive necessity for states, supporting a secular govtech-AI demand thesis.
insights.quote: governments that do not integrate AI-enabled services risk growing friction with increasingly AI-native populations
insights.locator: The frontier of AI capabilities is advancing quickly
